skill.name: zapier-make-patterns
skill.description: Guides users through Zapier and Make automation design, including platform selection, workflow patterns, error handling, and when to move from no-code tools to custom code.
skill.summary: Design reliable Zapier and Make automations with clear platform and workflow patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged inline backticks and weak-crypto substrings, but the reviewed file is a Markdown guidance document with no executable code. The line 67 detections are related skill names inside inline code formatting, and line 3 is descriptive prose. No prompt injection attempt, credential access, network exfiltration, or executable command path was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Inline Backticked Skill Names
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution at SKILL.md line 67. The line only lists related skill names in Markdown inline code formatting, such as workflow and backend topics. It does not contain executable shell or Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is Markdown prose with inline code spans only. There is no interpreter context, command invocation, variable interpolation, or user input path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Match
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns at SKILL.md lines 3 and 67. The reviewed text contains descriptive automation guidance and related skill labels, not cryptographic functions or algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both flagged locations are plain Markdown text. No cryptographic API, hashing call, encryption library, or algorithm selection is present.

content.user_title: Build Reliable Zapier and Make Workflows
content.value_statement: No-code automations can fail when platform limits, field mapping, and error handling are ignored. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design practical Zapier and Make workflows with clear tradeoffs.
actual_capabilities: Compares Zapier and Make for simple and complex automation needs.
Explains trigger-action, sequential, and conditional branching workflow patterns.
Identifies common no-code automation anti-patterns, including missing error handling.
Advises when a workflow should move from no-code tools to custom code.
Highlights platform reliability concerns such as app updates, duplicates, and operation counting.
limitations: Does not connect to Zapier, Make, or external services directly.
Does not create live Zaps, Make scenarios, or API credentials.
Does not verify current app integration availability or pricing.
Provides design guidance, not guaranteed platform-specific implementation steps.

use_cases.title: Choose an Automation Platform
use_cases.description: Compare Zapier and Make for a planned workflow based on complexity, app support, branching, and budget.

use_cases.title: Review an Existing Workflow
use_cases.description: Find weak points in a Zap or Make scenario before it causes duplicate records, broken data, or silent failures.

use_cases.title: Plan a Migration to Code
use_cases.description: Decide when a no-code automation has become too fragile or expensive and should move to a coded service.

prompt_templates.title: Pick the Right Platform
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to automate this process: [describe process]. Compare Zapier and Make for this workflow. Recommend one platform and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Zapier or Make workflow for [business goal]. Include triggers, actions, branches, field mappings, and error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this automation design for reliability risks: [paste workflow]. Identify brittle fields, duplicate risks, missing checks, and recovery steps.

prompt_templates.title: Decide When to Use Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this no-code workflow should stay in Zapier or Make, or move to custom code: [describe volume, logic, apps, and failures].

output_examples.input: Compare Zapier and Make for routing new form leads into a CRM with Slack alerts.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use Zapier if the routing is simple and the CRM integration is native. Use Make if lead routing needs several branches, data cleanup, or lower operation cost at scale.

output_examples.input: Audit a workflow that sends every paid invoice to accounting and a customer success channel.
output_examples.output: Key risks: duplicate invoice events, missing payment status checks, hardcoded channel names, and no retry path. Add idempotency checks and explicit error notifications.

output_examples.input: Decide whether a Make scenario should become a custom service.
output_examples.output: Move to code when the workflow has high volume, complex state, strict audit needs, or repeated platform workarounds. Keep it no-code for simple app-to-app coordination.

best_practices: Start with a clear trigger, required fields, and expected failure modes.
Use dropdown-selected fields and stable identifiers instead of typed labels.
Add duplicate handling, error alerts, and test records before production use.
anti_patterns: Building complex branching logic in Zapier when Make or code would be clearer.
Typing values into dropdown-backed fields instead of selecting supported options.
Ignoring operation counts, retries, and app update behavior during design.

faq.question: Does this skill build automations directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides planning, review, and architecture guidance for Zapier and Make workflows.

faq.question: Can it help choose between Zapier and Make?
faq.answer: Yes. It compares simplicity, branching needs, integration support, transformation work, and cost concerns.

faq.question: Is this useful for non-developers?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is written for workflow builders who may not write code.

faq.question: Can it review an existing Zap or scenario?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the workflow steps, fields, errors, and business goal for a reliability review.

faq.question: Does it know current Zapier and Make pricing?
faq.answer: No. Pricing and app limits can change, so users should verify current platform details.

faq.question: When should users move from no-code to code?
faq.answer: Move to code when logic, volume, state, audit needs, or maintenance cost exceed platform strengths.
